seoTitle: How to Check If Excel Is 32-Bit or 64-Bit
metaDescription: Quickly find whether your Microsoft Excel install is 32-bit or 64-bit using the built-in Account panel, useful before installing add-ins or plugins.
intro: Knowing whether your copy of Excel is 32-bit or 64-bit matters when you're troubleshooting crashes with large datasets, dealing with macros that won't compile, or picking the right installer for an add-in. Excel makes this information available in just a couple of clicks through its built-in account details panel.

steps.heading: Open the File menu
steps.body: Click File in the top-left corner of Excel to open the backstage view, which holds account and application information separate from your worksheet.

steps.heading: Go to the Account section
steps.body: From the backstage menu, select Account to see details about your Excel installation and Microsoft 365 subscription.

steps.heading: Open About Excel
steps.body: Click the About Excel button to open a details window that lists your exact version number along with whether the installation is 32-bit or 64-bit.

steps.heading: Read the build information
steps.body: The version details window displays the architecture directly next to the build number, giving you the exact information needed for compatibility checks with add-ins or macros.

faq.question: Why does it matter if Excel is 32-bit or 64-bit?
faq.answer: The 64-bit version can handle larger datasets and more memory, which matters for big workbooks, while some older add-ins only work with the 32-bit version.

faq.question: Can I switch between 32-bit and 64-bit Excel?
faq.answer: Switching requires uninstalling your current version and reinstalling the other architecture, since Microsoft 365 lets you choose which one to install.

faq.question: Which version should I choose for a new install?
faq.answer: Most users benefit from 64-bit unless they rely on a specific legacy add-in that only supports 32-bit.

faq.question: Does checking the bit version require any special permissions?
faq.answer: No, any user can view this information through the standard Account and About Excel screens without administrator access.
